summaryText: This guide is for BYU undergraduates considering graduate work in economics. It explains what PhD programs in economics are like (highly math- and research-intensive), who is a good fit, and what preparation is expected during undergrad. It emphasizes that most economics graduate study means pursuing a PhD, often after gaining pre-doctoral research experience rather than going straight from undergrad.
mathPrep: Graduate economics is very math-heavy. BYU recommends going well beyond calculus into proof-based real analysis and advanced linear algebra. Essential courses include MATH 341 (Theory of Analysis) and the 500-level econ theory/metrics sequence (ECON 580, 581, 582, 588). A math minor or major is strongly encouraged for students targeting top PhD programs.
researchPrep: Strong PhD candidates have meaningful research exposure as undergraduates. The guide stresses getting involved as a research assistant (RA) with faculty, attending seminars, and using courses like ECON 398 (Applied Econometrics) as a first taste of research. Curiosity, self-motivation, and comfort with coding (R, Python, Stata) are critical.
applicationChecklist: Decide if a PhD in economics is a good fit (math tolerance, interest in research, long time horizon).
Complete advanced math (through real analysis) and the 500-level econ theory/metrics sequence where possible.
Gain RA experience with faculty and, if possible, a pre-doctoral research position after graduation.
Build relationships with faculty who can write detailed, research-focused letters of recommendation.
Prepare for and take the GRE (if required) with strong quantitative scores.
Draft and refine a focused statement of purpose highlighting research interests and preparation.
Create a realistic, balanced list of PhD programs based on grades, math background, and RA experience.
